title: Network Devices, Topologies & SDN
summary: How hubs, switches, routers, firewalls, proxies, and load balancers differ, the physical/logical topologies that connect them, the three-tier campus design, and how SDN/NFV decouple control from forwarding.
content:
Network devices map to OSI layers, and knowing the layer tells you what the device can see. • A hub (Layer 1) is a dumb repeater: it floods every bit out all ports, creating one giant collision and broadcast domain (obsolete). • A bridge (Layer 2) was an early two-port device that learned MACs and split collision domains. • A switch (Layer 2) is a multiport bridge: it builds a MAC address table (CAM table) and forwards frames only to the correct port — every port is its own collision domain, while all ports share one broadcast domain (per VLAN). • A router (Layer 3) forwards packets between different IP networks/broadcast domains using a routing table and is the boundary of a broadcast domain. • A Layer 3 switch is a switch with routing silicon (ASICs) added — it does wire-speed inter-VLAN routing and is the workhorse of campus distribution/core.

Security and traffic devices sit at the edges. • A firewall enforces an allow/deny policy. A stateful firewall tracks the state of each connection (the session table) so return traffic for an established flow is automatically permitted. A Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) adds Layer 7 awareness: application identification, deep packet inspection, user-ID, IPS, and TLS inspection. • An IDS detects and alerts on malicious traffic but does not block it; it is typically out-of-band (a SPAN/mirror port copy). • An IPS sits inline and can drop malicious packets in real time — placement is the key exam point: IDS = passive/tap, IPS = inline.

Proxies and load balancers shape application traffic. • A forward proxy sits in front of internal clients reaching the Internet (web filtering, caching, anonymizing the client). • A reverse proxy sits in front of servers, terminating client connections on the servers' behalf (TLS offload, caching, hiding the back end). • A load balancer distributes incoming requests across a server pool using algorithms (round robin, least connections, weighted) and removes failed nodes via health checks — it provides scalability and high availability.

Wireless infrastructure scales through centralization. • An autonomous (fat) WAP holds its own config. • In enterprise/embassy deployments, lightweight (thin) APs are managed by a Wireless LAN Controller (WLC) that pushes SSIDs, channels, power levels, and security policy centrally and handles fast roaming.

Physical and logical topologies describe how nodes interconnect. • Star (hub-and-spoke at the LAN level): every node connects to a central switch — the dominant LAN topology; the central device is a single point of failure. • Mesh: nodes interconnect directly. Full mesh gives a path between every pair (n(n-1)/2 links — costly but maximally redundant); partial mesh connects only critical nodes. • Bus: a single shared backbone (legacy, easy to break). • Ring: each node connects to two neighbors, traffic circulates (FDDI/Token Ring, SONET rings). • Point-to-point: a dedicated link between exactly two endpoints (a WAN circuit). • Hub-and-spoke: branch sites (spokes) all connect back to a central hub site — common for embassies connecting to a ministry headquarters.

The enterprise campus uses a three-tier hierarchical model. • Access layer: where endpoints plug in (PoE, 802.1X, VLAN assignment). • Distribution (aggregation) layer: aggregates access switches, does inter-VLAN routing, applies ACLs/QoS policy. • Core layer: the high-speed, low-latency backbone that forwards as fast as possible and avoids policy logic. Smaller sites collapse distribution and core into one (collapsed core).

SDN and NFV change where intelligence lives. • Software-Defined Networking separates the control plane (the decision-making 'brain' — routing/topology) from the data/forwarding plane (the part that actually moves packets). A centralized SDN controller programs many switches via a southbound API (e.g., OpenFlow), and applications talk to the controller via a northbound API. This enables central, programmable, vendor-agnostic policy. • Network Function Virtualization (NFV) runs network functions (firewall, router, load balancer) as software/VMs on commodity x86 hardware instead of dedicated appliances, allowing rapid, elastic deployment. SDN steers the traffic; NFV provides the functions.
keyPoints: Hub = L1 (one collision + one broadcast domain); switch = L2 (per-port collision domains, one broadcast domain per VLAN); router/L3 switch = L3 (separates broadcast domains).
A Layer 3 switch does wire-speed inter-VLAN routing in hardware ASICs and anchors the campus core/distribution.
Stateful firewall tracks connection state so return traffic is auto-allowed; NGFW adds L7 app-ID, DPI, user-ID, and integrated IPS.
IDS is passive/out-of-band (alerts only); IPS is inline and can drop traffic — placement is the exam discriminator.
Forward proxy protects clients reaching out; reverse proxy fronts servers (TLS offload, caching, hides back end).
Load balancers distribute traffic (round robin, least connections, weighted) and use health checks for high availability.
Thin/lightweight APs are centrally managed by a Wireless LAN Controller; fat/autonomous APs are self-configured.
Star is the standard LAN topology; full mesh = n(n-1)/2 links for max redundancy; hub-and-spoke connects branches to a central site.
Three-tier model: access (endpoints/PoE/802.1X), distribution (aggregation, routing, ACLs/QoS), core (fast backbone, no policy).
SDN separates control plane (centralized brain) from data plane (forwarding); NFV runs network functions as software on commodity hardware.

flashcards.front: What two domains does a switch separate vs. share?
flashcards.back: Separates collision domains (one per port) but shares one broadcast domain per VLAN.

flashcards.front: What is the boundary of a broadcast domain?
flashcards.back: A router (Layer 3 interface) — or a Layer 3 switch's routed/VLAN interface.

flashcards.front: What does a Layer 3 switch add over a regular switch?
flashcards.back: Hardware (ASIC) routing — wire-speed inter-VLAN routing in addition to L2 switching.

flashcards.front: Stateful firewall vs. stateless?
flashcards.back: Stateful tracks connection state in a session table and auto-permits return traffic; stateless filters each packet against ACL rules independently.

flashcards.front: What does the 'NG' in NGFW add?
flashcards.back: Layer 7 application awareness: app-ID, deep packet inspection, user identity, integrated IPS, and TLS/SSL inspection.

flashcards.front: IDS vs. IPS placement and action?
flashcards.back: IDS = out-of-band (SPAN/tap), detects and alerts only. IPS = inline, can actively block/drop malicious traffic.

flashcards.front: Forward proxy vs. reverse proxy?
flashcards.back: Forward proxy sits in front of clients (outbound filtering/caching); reverse proxy sits in front of servers (TLS offload, caching, hides back end).

flashcards.front: Name three common load-balancing algorithms.
flashcards.back: Round robin, least connections, and weighted (round robin or least connections).

flashcards.front: What manages lightweight (thin) access points?
flashcards.back: A Wireless LAN Controller (WLC), which centralizes SSID, channel, power, and security policy.

flashcards.front: How many links in a full mesh of n nodes?
flashcards.back: n(n-1)/2 links.

flashcards.front: Which topology connects all branch sites to one central site?
flashcards.back: Hub-and-spoke (star at the WAN level) — common for embassies linking to a headquarters.

flashcards.front: Name the three layers of the hierarchical campus model.
flashcards.back: Access, distribution (aggregation), and core.

flashcards.front: Which campus layer should NOT run heavy policy/ACLs?
flashcards.back: The core — it is optimized purely for fast, low-latency forwarding.

flashcards.front: In SDN, what is separated from what?
flashcards.back: The control plane (centralized decision-making/routing logic) is separated from the data/forwarding plane (packet movement).

flashcards.front: Northbound vs. southbound API in SDN?
flashcards.back: Northbound: apps talk to the controller. Southbound: the controller programs the switches (e.g., OpenFlow).

flashcards.front: What is NFV?
flashcards.back: Network Function Virtualization — running network functions (firewall, router, LB) as software/VMs on commodity hardware instead of dedicated appliances.

title: WAN, Remote Access & the Internet Edge
summary: The circuits and technologies that connect sites over distance — leased lines, MPLS, Metro Ethernet, SD-WAN, broadband, cellular, and satellite — plus a high-level view of routing protocols, autonomous systems, BGP, CDNs, and demarcation equipment.
content:
A WAN connects geographically separated sites and is almost always provided by a carrier/service provider. Embassies are textbook WAN consumers: a mission abroad must reach its home ministry over leased or public infrastructure, often with strict availability and confidentiality requirements.

Dedicated and private WAN circuits give predictable, contracted bandwidth. • A leased line is a permanent point-to-point circuit (always-on, symmetric, no contention). • T1 (North America/Japan) = 1.544 Mbps over 24 channels; E1 (Europe/most of the world) = 2.048 Mbps over 32 channels — high-yield numbers for a diplomatic-context exam. T3 ≈ 45 Mbps, E3 ≈ 34 Mbps. • MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching) is a carrier technology that forwards traffic by short labels rather than IP lookups, building a private, any-to-any provider network with strong QoS and SLAs — long the gold standard for multi-site enterprises. • Metro Ethernet delivers Ethernet handoffs across a metropolitan area (simple, scalable, cost-effective).

SD-WAN is the modern overlay. • Software-Defined WAN abstracts and pools multiple underlying transports (MPLS, broadband, LTE) and uses centralized policy plus application-aware path selection to send each application over the best available link, with automatic failover. It reduces reliance on expensive MPLS and is increasingly used to connect missions resiliently over cheaper Internet links with encryption.

Broadband and last-mile options vary by location. • DSL runs data over copper phone lines (distance-sensitive; ADSL is asymmetric). • Cable uses DOCSIS over coax (shared local segment). • Fiber to the premises, often via GPON (Gigabit Passive Optical Network), uses passive splitters to share one fiber among many subscribers — high speed, the preferred modern access. • Cellular 4G LTE / 5G provides wireless WAN; 5G adds far higher throughput and low latency. Cellular is widely used as an automatic failover/backup link (and for rapid or temporary deployments). • Satellite backhaul (GEO or newer LEO constellations) reaches remote posts with no terrestrial infrastructure; GEO satellite has very high latency (~500–600 ms round trip) due to ~36,000 km orbit, while LEO greatly reduces that.

Routing protocols determine paths and divide into interior and exterior. • Interior Gateway Protocols run inside one administrative domain (autonomous system): RIP (legacy distance-vector, hop-count metric, max 15 hops); OSPF (open standard link-state, uses cost/bandwidth, fast convergence, scales well); EIGRP (Cisco advanced distance-vector, uses bandwidth and delay). • BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the exterior/path-vector protocol that runs between autonomous systems — it is the routing protocol of the Internet. An Autonomous System (AS) is a network under one administrative control identified by an AS number (ASN); BGP exchanges reachability between ASes and applies policy. • A default route (0.0.0.0/0, the 'gateway of last resort') is where a router sends any traffic that matches no more specific route — typically pointing at the ISP.

CDNs and edge services improve delivery. • A Content Delivery Network caches content on geographically distributed edge servers close to users, cutting latency and origin load — relevant for serving consular/public-facing web content globally.

Demarcation and edge equipment define responsibility boundaries. • The demarc (demarcation point) is where the provider's network ends and the customer's begins — the legal/operational boundary of responsibility. • CPE (Customer Premises Equipment) is the gear on the customer side (router, modem, ONT). • A CSU/DSU (Channel Service Unit / Data Service Unit) terminates a digital leased line (e.g., T1) and connects it to the customer router — the old equivalent of a modem for digital circuits.
keyPoints: T1 = 1.544 Mbps (24 channels, NA/Japan); E1 = 2.048 Mbps (32 channels, Europe/most of world).
MPLS forwards by labels (not IP lookups) to build a private any-to-any provider network with QoS/SLA guarantees.
SD-WAN pools multiple transports (MPLS/broadband/LTE) with central policy and application-aware path selection plus failover.
GPON (fiber) uses passive splitters to share one fiber among many subscribers; DSL is over copper, cable over coax (DOCSIS).
Cellular 4G/5G is commonly used as automatic WAN failover; 5G adds high throughput and low latency.
GEO satellite has ~500–600 ms round-trip latency (~36,000 km orbit); LEO constellations cut latency dramatically.
Interior gateway protocols: RIP (hop count, max 15), OSPF (link-state, cost), EIGRP (Cisco, bandwidth+delay).
BGP is the path-vector exterior protocol that routes between Autonomous Systems — the routing protocol of the Internet.
A default route (0.0.0.0/0) is the gateway of last resort for traffic matching no specific route.
Demarc = provider/customer boundary; CPE = customer-side gear; CSU/DSU terminates a digital leased line (e.g., T1) to the router.

flashcards.front: What is a leased line?
flashcards.back: A permanent, dedicated, always-on point-to-point circuit with symmetric, uncontended bandwidth.

flashcards.front: T1 vs. E1 speed and channels?
flashcards.back: T1 = 1.544 Mbps / 24 channels (NA/Japan); E1 = 2.048 Mbps / 32 channels (Europe/most of the world).

flashcards.front: How does MPLS forward traffic?
flashcards.back: By short labels rather than full IP-header lookups, creating a private any-to-any provider network with QoS/SLAs.

flashcards.front: What is SD-WAN?
flashcards.back: A software-defined overlay that pools multiple WAN transports and uses centralized, application-aware policy to pick the best path with automatic failover.

flashcards.front: What does GPON use to share a single fiber?
flashcards.back: Passive optical splitters serving many subscribers from one fiber (no powered equipment in the field).

flashcards.front: Why is GEO satellite latency so high?
flashcards.back: The satellite orbits ~36,000 km up, giving ~500–600 ms round-trip propagation delay; LEO orbits much lower and reduces this.

flashcards.front: Common use of cellular (4G/5G) in a WAN?
flashcards.back: Automatic backup/failover link when the primary circuit drops (and for rapid/temporary deployments).

flashcards.front: RIP's metric and limitation?
flashcards.back: Hop count, with a maximum of 15 hops (16 = unreachable) — limits network diameter; legacy distance-vector.

flashcards.front: OSPF type and metric?
flashcards.back: Open-standard link-state protocol; metric is cost, derived from interface bandwidth; fast convergence and scalable.

flashcards.front: What does BGP route between?
flashcards.back: Autonomous Systems (ASes) — it is the exterior path-vector protocol that powers Internet routing.

flashcards.front: What is an Autonomous System (AS)?
flashcards.back: A network under a single administrative control, identified by an AS number (ASN).

flashcards.front: What does a default route (0.0.0.0/0) do?
flashcards.back: Acts as the gateway of last resort — forwards any traffic not matching a more specific route, usually toward the ISP.

flashcards.front: What is the demarc (demarcation point)?
flashcards.back: The point where the service provider's responsibility ends and the customer's network begins.

flashcards.front: What does a CSU/DSU do?
flashcards.back: Terminates a digital leased line (e.g., T1/E1) and connects it to the customer's router — the digital-circuit equivalent of a modem.

flashcards.front: What problem does a CDN solve?
flashcards.back: It caches content on distributed edge servers near users to reduce latency and offload the origin server.

title: Network Services, Management & Monitoring
summary: The operational backbone of a managed network — NTP, SNMP, syslog, flow monitoring, QoS, DHCP relay, captive portals, 802.1X/NAC, certificate-based auth, and the SLAs/baselining/capacity-planning practices that keep service levels honest.
content:
A well-run embassy network depends as much on management and monitoring services as on the cabling and routers. These services provide time, telemetry, prioritization, and access control.

Accurate time underpins everything. • NTP (Network Time Protocol, UDP 123) synchronizes clocks across devices. Correct time is essential for log correlation, Kerberos/Active Directory authentication (which fails if clocks drift more than ~5 minutes), certificate validity, and scheduled jobs. NTP uses a hierarchy of stratum levels: stratum 0 is a reference clock (GPS/atomic), stratum 1 servers attach directly to it, and each lower stratum is one hop further.

SNMP gives device telemetry. • Simple Network Management Protocol uses a manager (NMS) that polls agents on devices over UDP 161; agents send unsolicited alerts called traps over UDP 162. • The MIB (Management Information Base) is the hierarchical database of manageable objects, each identified by an OID. • Versions matter for the exam: v1/v2c use plaintext community strings (insecure); v3 adds authentication and encryption and should be used in any secure/embassy environment.

Logging and flow data provide forensic and capacity insight. • Syslog (UDP 514) centralizes event messages to a collector. Severity levels run 0–7 from most to least severe: 0 Emergency, 1 Alert, 2 Critical, 3 Error, 4 Warning, 5 Notice, 6 Informational, 7 Debug (mnemonic: 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice cream Daily'). Lower number = higher severity. • NetFlow (Cisco) and the vendor-neutral sFlow export traffic flow records (who talked to whom, how much) — NetFlow accounts for every flow; sFlow statistically samples packets. They answer 'what is using the bandwidth?' rather than just 'how much.'

QoS prioritizes traffic when links are congested — vital for VoIP and video conferencing between missions. The QoS workflow: • Classification identifies traffic (by port, DSCP, app). • Marking tags packets (DSCP in the IP header, e.g., EF/Expedited Forwarding for voice; CoS at Layer 2). • Queuing places marked traffic into priority queues so latency-sensitive voice goes first. • Policing vs. shaping is a classic distinction: policing enforces a rate by dropping (or re-marking) excess traffic immediately, while shaping buffers excess traffic and releases it smoothly later (delays rather than drops). Use shaping toward a provider to smooth bursts; use policing to hard-cap a flow.

Addressing and onboarding services. • A DHCP relay (IP helper) forwards DHCP DISCOVER broadcasts across a router to a DHCP server on another subnet, so one central server can serve many VLANs (since broadcasts don't cross routers). • A captive portal intercepts a new client's first web request and forces a login/terms page (common on guest Wi-Fi).

Strong network access control authenticates the port itself. • 802.1X is port-based network access control with three roles: the supplicant (client), the authenticator (switch/AP), and the authentication server (RADIUS). Using EAP, it authenticates a device/user before the port is opened — the foundation of NAC (Network Access Control), which can also posture-check a device (patch level, AV) and quarantine non-compliant endpoints. • Certificate-based network auth (EAP-TLS) uses client and server X.509 certificates instead of passwords — phishing-resistant and standard for high-security/embassy wireless and wired access.

Service levels and planning keep performance accountable. • An SLA (Service Level Agreement) is a contract defining the guaranteed level of service — uptime/availability (e.g., 99.9% ≈ 8.76 hours downtime/year), latency, and resolution times, with penalties. • Baselining is measuring normal performance over time so anomalies and trends are visible. • Capacity planning uses baselines and growth trends to provision resources before they are exhausted, avoiding congestion and outages.
keyPoints: NTP (UDP 123) syncs clocks; uses strata (0 = reference clock); correct time is required for log correlation, Kerberos/AD, and certificates.
SNMP manager polls agents on UDP 161; agents send traps on UDP 162; the MIB is the object database (OIDs).
Use SNMPv3 (auth + encryption); v1/v2c use plaintext community strings and are insecure.
Syslog (UDP 514) severity 0–7: 0 Emergency (highest) → 7 Debug (lowest); lower number = more severe.
NetFlow/sFlow show WHO/WHAT is using bandwidth (flow records); NetFlow accounts all flows, sFlow samples.
QoS order: classify → mark (DSCP, EF for voice) → queue → police/shape.
Policing drops/re-marks excess immediately; shaping buffers and smooths excess (delay vs. drop).
DHCP relay (IP helper) forwards DHCP broadcasts across a router so one server covers multiple subnets/VLANs.
802.1X roles: supplicant (client), authenticator (switch/AP), authentication server (RADIUS); foundation of NAC.
EAP-TLS uses client/server certificates (no passwords) — phishing-resistant, standard for high-security access.

flashcards.front: What port and protocol does NTP use, and why does time matter?
flashcards.back: UDP 123. Accurate time is needed for log correlation, Kerberos/AD auth (fails past ~5 min drift), certificate validity, and scheduling.

flashcards.front: What is NTP stratum 0 vs. stratum 1?
flashcards.back: Stratum 0 is a reference clock (GPS/atomic); stratum 1 servers connect directly to a stratum 0 source.

flashcards.front: SNMP polling vs. traps — which ports?
flashcards.back: Manager polls agents on UDP 161; agents send unsolicited traps on UDP 162.

flashcards.front: What is an SNMP MIB?
flashcards.back: The Management Information Base — a hierarchical database of manageable objects, each identified by an OID.

flashcards.front: Which SNMP version is secure and why?
flashcards.back: v3 — it adds authentication and encryption; v1/v2c rely on plaintext community strings.

flashcards.front: Syslog severity 0 vs. 7?
flashcards.back: 0 = Emergency (most severe), 7 = Debug (least severe). Lower number = higher severity.

flashcards.front: What does NetFlow/sFlow tell you that SNMP bandwidth counters don't?
flashcards.back: Who is talking to whom and which applications are consuming bandwidth (flow records), not just total throughput.

flashcards.front: NetFlow vs. sFlow sampling?
flashcards.back: NetFlow accounts for every flow; sFlow statistically samples packets.

flashcards.front: List the QoS steps in order.
flashcards.back: Classification, marking (DSCP/CoS), queuing, then policing or shaping.

flashcards.front: Policing vs. shaping?
flashcards.back: Policing drops or re-marks excess traffic immediately (hard cap); shaping buffers and releases it smoothly (introduces delay, not drops).

flashcards.front: What DSCP marking is used for voice?
flashcards.back: EF (Expedited Forwarding) — priority handling for latency-sensitive VoIP.

flashcards.front: What does a DHCP relay / IP helper do?
flashcards.back: Forwards DHCP broadcast requests across a router to a DHCP server on another subnet, letting one server serve many VLANs.

flashcards.front: Name the three roles in 802.1X.
flashcards.back: Supplicant (client), authenticator (switch/AP), and authentication server (usually RADIUS).

flashcards.front: What is EAP-TLS and why prefer it?
flashcards.back: Certificate-based network authentication using client/server X.509 certs (no passwords) — phishing-resistant; ideal for high-security access.

flashcards.front: What is a captive portal?
flashcards.back: A page that intercepts a new client's first web request to force login or acceptance of terms (typical for guest Wi-Fi).

flashcards.front: What does '99.9% availability' roughly equal in downtime per year?
flashcards.back: About 8.76 hours of downtime per year.

title: Operating Systems, Virtualization & Endpoints
summary: Core Windows and Linux fundamentals (file systems, permissions, processes, configuration, CLI, boot), RAID levels, hypervisors and containers, VDI/thin clients, patch/imaging, Active Directory/LDAP/Group Policy, and backup types.
content:
Embassy IT runs a mix of Windows endpoints/servers and Linux servers, so an officer must speak both. The two OS families differ in file systems, permissions, configuration, and tooling.

File systems and permissions. • Windows uses NTFS (journaling, ACLs, encryption via EFS, compression, large file support); older/removable media may use FAT32/exFAT. • Linux commonly uses ext4 (also XFS, Btrfs). • Windows permissions are ACL-based (granular per-user/group: Read, Write, Modify, Full Control). • Linux uses the rwx model on owner/group/other, expressed in octal — e.g., chmod 755 = rwxr-xr-x (owner full, group/others read+execute), 644 = rw-r--r--. This octal mapping is a frequent exam item (r=4, w=2, x=1).

Configuration and services. • Windows stores configuration in the Registry, a hierarchical database (hives like HKLM, HKCU). • Linux stores configuration as plain-text files under /etc. • Background programs are 'services' in Windows (managed with services.msc / sc / Get-Service) and 'daemons' in Linux (managed with systemd: systemctl start/stop/enable). • Processes are inspected with Task Manager / tasklist (Windows) and ps/top/htop (Linux); each has a PID.

CLI basics. • Windows offers CMD and the more powerful PowerShell (object-based: Get-ChildItem, Copy-Item). • Linux uses shells like Bash (ls, cp, mv, rm, cat, grep, sudo for elevation; man for help). Knowing equivalents (dir≈ls, ipconfig≈ip addr/ifconfig) is exam-relevant.

Boot process. • Modern firmware is UEFI (replacing legacy BIOS) and pairs with the GPT partition scheme (vs. MBR), enabling Secure Boot and disks larger than 2 TB. The chain: firmware (UEFI/BIOS) → bootloader (Windows Boot Manager / GRUB on Linux) → kernel → OS/services/login.

RAID provides redundancy and/or performance by combining disks. • RAID 0 (striping): splits data across disks for speed and full capacity but NO redundancy — one disk fails, all data lost. • RAID 1 (mirroring): identical copies on two disks; survives one failure; 50% capacity. • RAID 5 (striping with distributed parity): needs ≥3 disks, survives one disk failure, good capacity efficiency (loses one disk's worth to parity). • RAID 6 (double parity): needs ≥4 disks, survives TWO simultaneous failures. • RAID 10 (1+0, mirror + stripe): needs ≥4 disks, combines speed and redundancy, 50% capacity — preferred for performance-critical databases. RAID is availability, not backup.

Virtualization and containers. • A hypervisor abstracts hardware to run multiple VMs. Type 1 (bare-metal) runs directly on hardware (ESXi, Hyper-V, Proxmox/KVM) — used for servers, efficient and secure. Type 2 (hosted) runs on top of a host OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation) — used on desktops. • A VM virtualizes hardware and includes a full guest OS (heavier, strong isolation). A container virtualizes the OS and shares the host kernel, packaging just the app and its dependencies (lightweight, fast, portable; Docker/Kubernetes) — but weaker isolation than a VM.

Endpoints and centralized desktops. • A thin client is a minimal device that connects to a central server for its desktop/apps. • VDI (Virtual Desktop Infrastructure) hosts user desktops as VMs in the data center, streamed to endpoints — keeps data centrally controlled (attractive for security/embassy use where no data should reside on the endpoint).

Fleet management. • Patch management is the disciplined process of testing and deploying updates to fix vulnerabilities (Windows Update/WSUS, package managers). • Imaging deploys a standardized OS 'gold image' to many machines quickly (clone/sysprep), ensuring consistency.

Directory and policy. • Active Directory (AD) is Microsoft's directory service for centralized identity, authentication (Kerberos), and resource management; it is built on LDAP (the open directory access protocol, port 389; LDAPS 636) and DNS. • Group Policy (GPO) centrally enforces configuration and security settings across users and computers in AD (password policy, drive mappings, software). These let one administrator govern an entire mission's fleet.

Backup strategy. • Full backup copies all data (slow, large, fastest restore). • Incremental backup copies only data changed since the last backup of any type (fast/small backups, slower restore — needs the full plus every incremental). • Differential backup copies all data changed since the last FULL backup (grows over time, restore needs only the full plus the latest differential). Remember the 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offsite.
keyPoints: NTFS (Windows, ACLs/journaling) vs. ext4 (Linux); Linux permissions use rwx octal — chmod 755 = rwxr-xr-x (r=4, w=2, x=1).
Windows config = Registry (HKLM/HKCU hives); Linux config = plain-text files under /etc.
Services (Windows, systemctl/sc) = daemons (Linux); PowerShell is object-based, Bash is text-based.
Boot chain: UEFI/BIOS firmware → bootloader (Boot Manager / GRUB) → kernel → OS; UEFI+GPT enable Secure Boot and >2 TB disks.
RAID 0 = striping, no redundancy; RAID 1 = mirror (1 failure); RAID 5 = parity, ≥3 disks (1 failure); RAID 6 = ≥4 disks (2 failures); RAID 10 = mirror+stripe, ≥4 disks.
Type 1 hypervisor runs on bare metal (ESXi/Hyper-V) for servers; Type 2 runs on a host OS (VirtualBox) for desktops.
VM = full guest OS, strong isolation, heavy; container = shares host kernel, lightweight, faster, weaker isolation.
VDI hosts desktops centrally as VMs (data stays in the data center); thin clients are minimal endpoints to access them.
Active Directory uses Kerberos and is built on LDAP (389 / LDAPS 636) and DNS; Group Policy (GPO) enforces settings fleet-wide.
Backups: full (all), incremental (since last backup of any type), differential (since last full); follow 3-2-1 rule.

flashcards.front: Default file systems: Windows vs. Linux?
flashcards.back: Windows = NTFS (ACLs, journaling, EFS); Linux = commonly ext4 (also XFS/Btrfs).

flashcards.front: What does chmod 755 mean?
flashcards.back: rwxr-xr-x — owner read/write/execute, group and others read/execute (r=4, w=2, x=1).

flashcards.front: Where do Windows vs. Linux store configuration?
flashcards.back: Windows in the Registry (hives like HKLM/HKCU); Linux in plain-text files under /etc.

flashcards.front: Service vs. daemon — and how to manage on Linux?
flashcards.back: Background programs are 'services' (Windows) and 'daemons' (Linux); on modern Linux, manage with systemctl (start/stop/enable).

flashcards.front: UEFI+GPT advantages over BIOS+MBR?
flashcards.back: Secure Boot, faster boot, and support for disks larger than 2 TB (more partitions).

flashcards.front: RAID 0 vs. RAID 1?
flashcards.back: RAID 0 = striping for speed/capacity with no redundancy (any disk loss = total loss); RAID 1 = mirroring, survives one disk failure, 50% usable.

flashcards.front: RAID 5 vs. RAID 6 fault tolerance and minimum disks?
flashcards.back: RAID 5: ≥3 disks, survives 1 failure (single parity); RAID 6: ≥4 disks, survives 2 failures (double parity).

flashcards.front: What is RAID 10 and its trade-off?
flashcards.back: A stripe of mirrors (≥4 disks) giving both speed and redundancy, but only 50% usable capacity; favored for databases.

flashcards.front: Type 1 vs. Type 2 hypervisor?
flashcards.back: Type 1 runs bare-metal on hardware (ESXi, Hyper-V) for servers; Type 2 runs on a host OS (VirtualBox, Workstation) for desktops.

flashcards.front: VM vs. container key difference?
flashcards.back: A VM includes a full guest OS (heavy, strong isolation); a container shares the host kernel and packages just the app (light, fast, weaker isolation).

flashcards.front: What is VDI and why is it security-friendly?
flashcards.back: Virtual Desktop Infrastructure hosts desktops as VMs in the data center and streams them; no sensitive data resides on the endpoint.

flashcards.front: What protocol is Active Directory built on, and what port?
flashcards.back: LDAP — TCP 389 (LDAPS 636); AD also relies on Kerberos and DNS.

flashcards.front: What does Group Policy (GPO) do?
flashcards.back: Centrally enforces configuration and security settings on AD users and computers (passwords, mappings, software).

flashcards.front: Incremental vs. differential backup?
flashcards.back: Incremental copies data changed since the LAST backup of any type (small backups, restore needs full + all increments); differential copies data changed since the last FULL backup (restore needs only full + latest differential).

flashcards.front: What is the 3-2-1 backup rule?
flashcards.back: Keep 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy stored offsite.

flashcards.front: What is imaging in fleet management?
flashcards.back: Deploying a standardized 'gold image' OS build to many machines quickly (clone/sysprep) for consistency.
